risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomized (stratified by hospital site) to receive either enteral rosuvastatin or placebo" Comment: The study explicitly states patients were randomized, and the added detail that randomization was stratified by hospital site is a context clue consistent with a proper random allocation process. Although the exact sequence-generation mechanism is not spelled out here, the description is sufficient to support low risk for random sequence generation.

Summary: The report states that patients were randomized and that the trial was placebo-controlled and triple-blinded, but it gives no explicit description of how the upcoming assignment was concealed from the person enrolling participants. There is no mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, identical drug containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge. Comment: Because sequence generation/blinding is described but allocation concealment is not, this domain remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Masking / blinding: triple (participant, care provider, investigator)" and "Patients were randomized ... to receive either enteral rosuvastatin or placebo"; "At 6- and 12-month follow-up, research personnel, blinded to treatment allocation, completed study assessments". Comment: The study explicitly states triple masking, including participants and care providers, and used placebo control. This makes knowledge of allocation by participants/personnel unlikely, so performance bias is low across the study outcomes overall.

Quote: "At 6- and 12-month follow-up, research personnel, blinded to treatment allocation, completed study assessments via phone" and "For patients undergoing in-person physical performance assessments at 6- and 12-month follow-up, research staff, blinded to treatment allocation, ... completed testing" Comment: The outcome assessors were research personnel/research staff and were explicitly blinded to allocation. The main outcomes included patient-reported SF-36, EQ-5D, HADS, and performance-based physical tests; because the assessors conducting follow-up assessments and testing were blinded, and the measurements were either standardized patient-reported instruments or objective performance tests, knowledge of allocation would be unlikely to bias outcome measurement.

Quote: "At 6- and 12-month follow-up, respectively, 238 and 237 survivors were eligible for follow-up, and 233 (98%) and 220 (93%) were assessed" and "rates of missed follow-up assessments were similar between the randomized groups, and were very low" Comment: Follow-up was essentially complete among eligible survivors, with only small and apparently balanced loss at 6 and 12 months; the paper explicitly states missed assessments were very low and similar between groups, so attrition is unlikely to have biased the outcome results.
